entities.description: A publicly viewable OCR/TEI encoding of Wagner's Middle English text and apparatus. Its item-specific header calls the OCR uncorrected, says that no content editing was performed, and describes the markup as TEI in Libraries Level 1 with links to page images.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AAW7316.0001.001
notes: The XML language field says German, but direct inspection shows that the encoded poem is Middle English, the apparatus and appendix are German, and the closing manuscript colophon is Latin.
The file has 188 PB elements and links page milestones to U-M page images; this count is an encoding metric, not the printed pagination.

bibliographic.citation: Tundale: Das mittelenglische Gedicht über die Vision des Tundalus, auf Grund von vier Handschriften mit Einleitung und Anmerkungen, ed. Albrecht Wagner (Halle an der Saale: Max Niemeyer, 1893).
bibliographic.title: Tundale: Das mittelenglische Gedicht über die Vision des Tundalus

entities.label: Wagner 1893 edition of Tundale
notes: Wagner chose R as his principal base after line 63, supplied R's missing opening from C, and corrected R from A, B, C, and the Latin source.
Everything in the edited text not taken from R is printed in italics. Expanded abbreviations are italic within roman text and roman within italic supplied text.
The edition includes a German introduction (pp. III–XXXIX), the poem and apparatus (pp. 1–137), a short Nachtrag supplying omitted B variants, and concluding German notes (pp. 138–146).

rights.basis: The underlying work is medieval, and Wagner's edition was published in 1893, well beyond the maximum 95-year U.S. term described by the U.S. Copyright Office for pre-1978 protected works; U-M independently labels this item public domain.
rights.component: The anonymous medieval Middle English work and Wagner's 1893 printed edition

entities.description: Five parchment leaves bound at the end of a later paper volume preserve disordered fragments of the Middle English Tundale. Wagner records lines 2307–2326 followed by 115–386 on the first fragment and lines 700–1165 on the second.

entities.description: A fifth Middle English witness, not identified or collated by Wagner, in the composite Delamere Chaucer. The Tundale fragment occupies fols. 166v–175v.

dateStatements.label: composition or recorded vision
dateStatements.notes: The Latin work itself dates the vision to 1149; modern scholarship treats the date as close but distinguishes the narrated event from the act of writing.

entities.description: The Latin otherworld vision attributed to the Irish monk Marcus at Regensburg and dated within the work to 1149. It is the ultimate narrative source of the Middle English poem, although the immediate recension or vernacular intermediary remains disputed.
entities.label: Marcus of Regensburg, Visio Tnugdali

reviewNotes: The cataloged work is the anonymous Middle English verse version, not Marcus's Latin Visio Tnugdali, Wagner's German-language editorial matter, or any one manuscript witness.
Wagner's edition is multi-witness and materially eclectic: it is not a facsimile or a diplomatic transcription of Royal 17 B XLIII.

The current NLS digital object is a complete public color facsimile of Adv.MS.19.3.1; the BSB object is a complete public scan of Wagner's 1893 print edition. Their reuse terms are recorded separately.
The CELT Latin text and the modern METS edition are comparison resources, not ancestors of the repository encoding.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy or version of University of Michigan CME item AAW7316.0001.001, an uncorrected OCR encoding of Albrecht Wagner's 1893 critical edition of the anonymous Middle English Vision of Tundale. Wagner based lines 64–2354 primarily on British Library Royal MS 17 B XLIII (R), supplied the lost opening from Cotton MS Caligula A II (C), collated those witnesses with National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.19.3.1 (A) and Bodleian MS Ashmole 1491 (B), and used his 1882 Latin edition to assess readings. A fifth Middle English witness, now Beinecke Takamiya MS 32, was not identified or used by Wagner. The electronic item omits Wagner's introduction and concluding commentary but retains the edited poem, its dense variant apparatus, and a short appendix.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.region: England; rhyme-based Northern English dialect localization; narrative set in Ireland
catalogSummary.title: The Vision of Tundale

catalogingSubject.label: The Vision of Tundale
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English verse version of Tundale's otherworld journey, represented in the repository by U-M's encoding of Albrecht Wagner's 1893 critical text. This cataloging subject excludes Marcus's twelfth-century Latin Visio Tnugdali, each individual manuscript witness, Wagner's modern German introduction and commentary, and U-M's OCR/TEI encoding as distinct lineage layers.

notes: The Getty authority record gives circa 1400. Wagner argued for the beginning of the fifteenth century, perhaps still the fourteenth; this record does not normalize an approximate date to an exact year.
This is a narrated event date inherited from the Latin source, not the Middle English translation date.

scope.description: Date assigned within the narrative to Tundale's vision in Ireland

extent.description: One continuous verse work in Wagner's edition. The repository XML contains 2,354 L elements and no LG elements; this is an encoding metric for Wagner's eclectic text, not a universal line count across witnesses or modern editions.

formStatements.description: A narrative poem in short rhyming couplets, often described as octosyllabic but more cautiously read as flexible four-stress lines.

genres.label: Visionary writing
notes: Tundale's apparent death and soul journey make ‘otherworld vision’ more precise than treating the poem simply as a dream vision.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: German apparatus and a Latin witness colophon belong to editorial or manuscript layers and do not make the cataloged medieval work multilingual.
The repository XML's German language declaration describes the edition's apparatus poorly and does not override direct inspection of the Middle English poem.
The 1149 date belongs to the narrated vision and Latin source tradition; the 1451 colophon dates the Royal witness; 1893 dates Wagner's edition; and 2006 dates U-M's header creation.

The 2,354-line extent is specific to Wagner's eclectic text and this XML representation. Mearns reports 2,320 lines for Cotton, while the Advocates' version printed by Foster has a different lineation.
No single manuscript's scribal dialect, date, rights status, or internal headings are assigned wholesale to the abstract Middle English work.

next_steps: Consult Eileen Gardiner's 1980 article on the Middle English translation and Rodney Mearns's 1985 parallel Latin edition.
Do not promote either competing route from a note to a definitive relation without textual comparison.
openQuestions.question: Was the Middle English poem translated directly from a Latin recension, especially the Vincent of Beauvais tradition, or through an Anglo-Norman intermediary?

next_steps: Consult Gardiner's 1982 dating article and Mearns's language chapter.
Keep witness production dates and scribal dialects separate from the reconstructed language of the work.
openQuestions.question: Can the circa-1400 translation date and Wagner's Northern English rhyme-based localization be refined or revised using current linguistic scholarship?

next_steps: Compare rubrics and transitions across all five witnesses in Gardiner and Mearns.
Retain summary-level structure until the divisions can be shown to belong to the work rather than one witness.
openQuestions.question: Do the ten Passus, seven Gaudia, and Reversio Anime headings in Adv.MS.19.3.1 preserve authorial structure or a witness-specific scribal organization?

notes: This cultural-origin statement does not identify a specific place of composition or copying.
Wagner's localization is based on rhymes across an eclectic four-witness tradition; none of the four scribal witnesses preserves the reconstructed original dialect unchanged.

scope.description: Reconstructed language of the poem from rhyme evidence

notes: The Middle English version identifies Ireland but omits Marcus's more specific Cork setting.
Richard Heeg is the scribe of one later witness, and Marcus is author of the Latin source work; neither should be promoted to author of the anonymous Middle English poem.
The exact immediate source—Latin recension or Anglo-Norman intermediary—remains disputed.

scope.description: Translation or vernacular adaptation of the Tundale narrative
Verse reshaping, selection, and mitigation of some source passages
Four-witness critical text with comparison to the Latin source

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English verse retelling of the Vision of Tundale. Tundale, a wealthy and violent Irish knight, collapses at a meal and appears dead; separated from his body, his soul is guided by an angel through escalating punishments for sin, Hell and Lucifer, milder purgatorial states, earthly-paradisal joys, and a glimpse of Heaven. Returned to life, he confesses, receives the sacrament, gives away his goods, preaches what he has seen, and lives penitently. The poem makes divine justice, mercy, punishment, and conversion central to its account of the Christian afterlife.

This record describes the medieval Middle English poem. Wagner's German critical apparatus and U-M's OCR/TEI are described as source and encoding layers in the linked lineage manifest; Marcus's Latin Visio Tnugdali is a related source work, not the same cataloging unit.

Wagner's edited poem is continuous rather than divided into stable named sections. The Advocates' witness uniquely organizes the journey as ten Passus, seven Gaudia, and a Reversio Anime; because the other Middle English manuscripts lack those headings, this record does not impose that witness-specific segmentation on the whole work.

notes: The poem's manuscripts and modern scholarship also use Tondale, Tundal, and related forms.
This is an edition-layer title, not evidence that the medieval work was composed in German.

scope.description: Title of Wagner's immediate 1893 source edition

titles.value: Tundale: Das mittelenglische Gedicht über die Vision des Tundalus

publication.embeddedTitle: TUNDALE. — DAS MITTELENGLISCHE GEDICHT ÜBER DIE VISION DES TUNDALUS

work.regionDisplay: England; rhyme-based Northern English dialect localization; narrative set in Ireland
work.summary: An anonymous Middle English verse retelling of the Vision of Tundale. Tundale, a wealthy and violent Irish knight, collapses at a meal and appears dead; separated from his body, his soul is guided by an angel through escalating punishments for sin, Hell and Lucifer, milder purgatorial states, earthly-paradisal joys, and a glimpse of Heaven. Returned to life, he confesses, receives the sacrament, gives away his goods, preaches what he has seen, and lives penitently. The poem makes divine justice, mercy, punishment, and conversion central to its account of the Christian afterlife.
